commentaries.author: Cyprian of Carthage
commentaries.content: He was not that Light, but was sent to bear witness of that Light. Cyprian of Carthage Thus, moreover, John also in his epistle warns us, and says, "If we say that we have no sin, we deceive ourselves, and the truth is not in us; but if we confess our sins, the Lord is faithful and just to forgive us our sins. And again, in his epistle, John lays it down, and says, "If we say that we have no sin, we deceive ourselves, and the truth is not in us. Also in the Epistle of John: "If we say that we have no sin, we deceive ourselves, and the truth is not in us.
commentaries.content_en: He was not that Light, but was sent to bear witness of that Light. Cyprian of Carthage Thus, moreover, John also in his epistle warns us, and says, "If we say that we have no sin, we deceive ourselves, and the truth is not in us; but if we confess our sins, the Lord is faithful and just to forgive us our sins. And again, in his epistle, John lays it down, and says, "If we say that we have no sin, we deceive ourselves, and the truth is not in us. Also in the Epistle of John: "If we say that we have no sin, we deceive ourselves, and the truth is not in us.
commentaries.content_pt: Ele não era essa Luz, mas foi enviado para dar testemunho dessa Luz. Cipriano de Cartago Assim, além disso, João também em sua epístola nos adverte e diz: "Se dissermos que não temos pecado, a nós mesmos nos enganamos, e a verdade não está em nós; mas se confessarmos nossos pecados, o Senhor é fiel e justo para nos perdoar os pecados. E novamente, em sua epístola, João afirma: "Se dissermos que não temos pecado, a nós mesmos nos enganamos, e a verdade não está em nós. Também na Epístola de João: "Se dissermos que não temos pecado, a nós mesmos nos enganamos, e a verdade não está em nós.

commentaries.author: Cyril of Alexandria
commentaries.content: The Baptist having esteemed desert-abodes above the haunts of the cities, and having shewn forth an unwonted persistence in exercise of virtue, and having mounted to the very summit of the righteousness attainable by man, was most rightly wondered at, and even by some imagined to be Christ Himself. And indeed the rulers of the Jews led by his achievements in virtue to some such notion, send some to him bidding them to inquire if he be the Christ. The blessed Evangelist then not ignorant of the things that were by many bruited of him, of necessity puts, He was not the Light, that he might both uproot the error as to this, and again build up some weight of credence to him who was sent from God for a witness. For how is he not eminent exceedingly, how is he not every way worthy of marvel, who is so clad with great virtue and so illustrious in righteousness as to imitate Christ Himself, and by the choice beauty of his piety, to be even imagined to be the Light Itself. He was not then, says he, the Light, but sent to bear witness of the Light. In saying the Light, with the addition of the article, he shews that it is really one: for so it is in truth. For that both the blessed Baptist and each of the other saints, may be rightly called light we will not deny, seeing that it is said of them by our Saviour, Ye are the light of the world. And again it is said of the holy Baptist, I have ordained a lamp for My Christ, and, He was a burning and a shining light, and ye were willing for a season to rejoice in his light. But even though the saints be light, and the Baptist a lamp, we are not ignorant of the grace that was given them and of their supply from the Light. For neither is the light in the lamp its own, nor the illumination in the saints, but they are rendered bright and lightsome by the enlightening of the Truth and are lights in the world, holding forth the word of life. And what is the Life, whose word they holding forth are called light, save surely the Only-Begotten, Who saith, I am the Life. Therefore, One of a truth is That Which is verily Light, lighting, not enlightened: and by participation of the One, whatever is called light, will be so deemed of by imitation of It.
commentaries.content_en: The Baptist having esteemed desert-abodes above the haunts of the cities, and having shewn forth an unwonted persistence in exercise of virtue, and having mounted to the very summit of the righteousness attainable by man, was most rightly wondered at, and even by some imagined to be Christ Himself. And indeed the rulers of the Jews led by his achievements in virtue to some such notion, send some to him bidding them to inquire if he be the Christ. The blessed Evangelist then not ignorant of the things that were by many bruited of him, of necessity puts, He was not the Light, that he might both uproot the error as to this, and again build up some weight of credence to him who was sent from God for a witness. For how is he not eminent exceedingly, how is he not every way worthy of marvel, who is so clad with great virtue and so illustrious in righteousness as to imitate Christ Himself, and by the choice beauty of his piety, to be even imagined to be the Light Itself. He was not then, says he, the Light, but sent to bear witness of the Light. In saying the Light, with the addition of the article, he shews that it is really one: for so it is in truth. For that both the blessed Baptist and each of the other saints, may be rightly called light we will not deny, seeing that it is said of them by our Saviour, Ye are the light of the world. And again it is said of the holy Baptist, I have ordained a lamp for My Christ, and, He was a burning and a shining light, and ye were willing for a season to rejoice in his light. But even though the saints be light, and the Baptist a lamp, we are not ignorant of the grace that was given them and of their supply from the Light. For neither is the light in the lamp its own, nor the illumination in the saints, but they are rendered bright and lightsome by the enlightening of the Truth and are lights in the world, holding forth the word of life. And what is the Life, whose word they holding forth are called light, save surely the Only-Begotten, Who saith, I am the Life. Therefore, One of a truth is That Which is verily Light, lighting, not enlightened: and by participation of the One, whatever is called light, will be so deemed of by imitation of It.
commentaries.content_pt: O Batista, tendo estimado os desertos acima dos hábitos das cidades, e tendo demonstrado uma persistência incomum no exercício da virtude, e tendo alcançado o próprio cume da justiça acessível ao homem, foi admirado de maneira justa, e até mesmo por alguns imaginado ser o próprio Cristo. E, de fato, os governantes dos judeus, levados por suas realizações em virtude a tal noção, enviam alguns a ele, pedindo que perguntem se ele é o Cristo. O bendito Evangelista, então, não ignorando as coisas que muitos diziam a seu respeito, necessariamente coloca: Ele não era a Luz, para que tanto erradicasse o erro a esse respeito, quanto novamente construísse algum peso de credibilidade para aquele que foi enviado por Deus como testemunha. Pois como ele não é extremamente eminente, como ele não é de toda forma digno de admiração, que está tão revestido de grande virtude e tão ilustre em justiça a ponto de imitar o próprio Cristo, e pela beleza escolhida de sua piedade, ser até mesmo imaginado como a própria Luz? Ele não era, então, diz ele, a Luz, mas enviado para dar testemunho da Luz. Ao dizer a Luz, com a adição do artigo, ele mostra que é realmente uma: pois assim é na verdade. Pois que tanto o bendito Batista quanto cada um dos outros santos podem ser chamados de luz, não negaremos, visto que é dito deles por nosso Salvador: Vós sois a luz do mundo. E novamente é dito do santo Batista: Eu ordenei uma lâmpada para Meu Cristo, e, Ele era uma luz ardente e resplandecente, e vós estivestes dispostos por um tempo a alegrar-vos em sua luz. Mas mesmo que os santos sejam luz, e o Batista uma lâmpada, não ignoramos a graça que lhes foi dada e de seu suprimento da Luz. Pois nem a luz na lâmpada é sua própria, nem a iluminação nos santos, mas eles são tornados brilhantes e luminosos pela iluminação da Verdade e são luzes no mundo, apresentando a palavra da vida. E o que é a Vida, cuja palavra eles apresentam e são chamados de luz, senão certamente o Unigênito, que diz: Eu sou a Vida. Portanto, Um, na verdade, é Aquilo que é verdadeiramente Luz, iluminando, não iluminado: e pela participação do Um, tudo o que é chamado de luz, será assim considerado pela imitação d'Ele.

commentaries.author: George Leo Haydock
commentaries.content: He; that is John the Baptist, was not the true light: but the word was the true light. In the translation, it is necessary to express that the word was the true light, lest any one should think that John the Baptist was this light. (Witham)
commentaries.content_en: He; that is John the Baptist, was not the true light: but the word was the true light. In the translation, it is necessary to express that the word was the true light, lest any one should think that John the Baptist was this light. (Witham)
commentaries.content_pt: Ele; isto é, João Batista, não era a verdadeira luz: mas a palavra era a verdadeira luz. Na tradução, é necessário expressar que a palavra era a verdadeira luz, para que ninguém pense que João Batista era essa luz. (Witham)

commentaries.author: John Chrysostom
commentaries.content: Now if he did not introduce this as setting himself against this suspicion, then the expression is absolutely superfluous, and tautology rather than elucidation of his teaching. For why, after having said that he was sent to bear witness of that Light, does he again say, He was not that Light. (He says it,) not loosely or without reason; but, because, for the most part, among ourselves, the person witnessing is held to be greater, and generally more trustworthy than the person witnessed of; therefore, that none might suspect this in the case of John, at once from the very beginning he removes this evil suspicion, and having torn it up by the roots, shows who this is that bears witness, and who is He who is witnessed of, and what an interval there is between the witnessed of, and the bearer of witness. And after having done this, and shown His incomparable superiority, he afterwards proceeds fearlessly to the narrative which remains; and after carefully removing whatever strange (ideas) might secretly harbor in the minds of the simpler sort, so instills into all easily and without impediment the word of doctrine in its proper order. Let us pray then, that henceforth with the revelation of these thoughts and rightness of doctrine, we may have also a pure life and bright conversation, since these things profit nothing unless good works be present with us. For though we have all faith and all knowledge of the Scriptures, yet if we be naked and destitute of the protection derived from (holy) living, there is nothing to hinder us from being hurried into the fire of hell, and burning for ever in the unquenchable flame. For as they who have done good shall rise to life everlasting, so they who have dared the contrary shall rise to everlasting punishment, which never has an end. Let us then manifest all eagerness not to mar the gain which accrues to us from a right faith by the vileness of our actions, but becoming well-pleasing to Him by these also, boldly to look on Christ. No happiness can be equal to this. And may it come to pass, that we all having obtained what has been mentioned, may do all to the glory of God; to whom, with the Only-Begotten Son and the Holy Ghost, be glory for ever and ever.
commentaries.content_en: Now if he did not introduce this as setting himself against this suspicion, then the expression is absolutely superfluous, and tautology rather than elucidation of his teaching. For why, after having said that he was sent to bear witness of that Light, does he again say, He was not that Light. (He says it,) not loosely or without reason; but, because, for the most part, among ourselves, the person witnessing is held to be greater, and generally more trustworthy than the person witnessed of; therefore, that none might suspect this in the case of John, at once from the very beginning he removes this evil suspicion, and having torn it up by the roots, shows who this is that bears witness, and who is He who is witnessed of, and what an interval there is between the witnessed of, and the bearer of witness. And after having done this, and shown His incomparable superiority, he afterwards proceeds fearlessly to the narrative which remains; and after carefully removing whatever strange (ideas) might secretly harbor in the minds of the simpler sort, so instills into all easily and without impediment the word of doctrine in its proper order. Let us pray then, that henceforth with the revelation of these thoughts and rightness of doctrine, we may have also a pure life and bright conversation, since these things profit nothing unless good works be present with us. For though we have all faith and all knowledge of the Scriptures, yet if we be naked and destitute of the protection derived from (holy) living, there is nothing to hinder us from being hurried into the fire of hell, and burning for ever in the unquenchable flame. For as they who have done good shall rise to life everlasting, so they who have dared the contrary shall rise to everlasting punishment, which never has an end. Let us then manifest all eagerness not to mar the gain which accrues to us from a right faith by the vileness of our actions, but becoming well-pleasing to Him by these also, boldly to look on Christ. No happiness can be equal to this. And may it come to pass, that we all having obtained what has been mentioned, may do all to the glory of God; to whom, with the Only-Begotten Son and the Holy Ghost, be glory for ever and ever.
commentaries.content_pt: Agora, se ele não introduziu isso como se opusesse a essa suspeita, então a expressão é absolutamente supérflua, e uma tautologia em vez de uma elucidação de seu ensino. Pois por que, depois de ter dito que foi enviado para dar testemunho dessa Luz, ele novamente diz: Ele não era essa Luz? (Ele diz isso), não de maneira solta ou sem razão; mas, porque, na maior parte, entre nós, a pessoa que testemunha é considerada maior, e geralmente mais confiável do que a pessoa de quem se dá testemunho; portanto, para que ninguém pudesse suspeitar disso no caso de João, desde o início ele remove essa má suspeita, e tendo-a arrancado pela raiz, mostra quem é aquele que dá testemunho, e quem é Aquele que é testemunhado, e quão grande é o intervalo entre o testemunhado e o portador do testemunho. E, após ter feito isso, e mostrado Sua incomparável superioridade, ele prossegue destemidamente para a narrativa que resta; e, após remover cuidadosamente quaisquer ideias estranhas que pudessem secretamente habitar nas mentes dos mais simples, assim instila em todos facilmente e sem impedimento a palavra da doutrina em sua devida ordem. Oremos, então, para que doravante, com a revelação desses pensamentos e a correção da doutrina, possamos também ter uma vida pura e uma conversa brilhante, uma vez que essas coisas não servem para nada, a menos que boas obras estejam presentes conosco. Pois, embora tenhamos toda a fé e todo o conhecimento das Escrituras, se estivermos nus e desprovidos da proteção derivada da vida (santa), nada impede que sejamos apressados para o fogo do inferno e queimemos para sempre na chama inextinguível. Pois assim como aqueles que fizeram o bem ressuscitarão para a vida eterna, assim aqueles que ousaram o contrário ressuscitarão para o castigo eterno, que nunca tem fim. Manifestemos, então, toda a disposição para não macular o ganho que nos advém de uma fé correta pela vileza de nossas ações, mas, tornando-nos agradáveis a Ele também por meio destas, olhemos corajosamente para Cristo. Nenhuma felicidade pode ser igual a esta. E que aconteça que todos nós, tendo obtido o que foi mencionado, possamos fazer tudo para a glória de Deus; a quem, com o Unigênito Filho e o Espírito Santo, seja a glória para sempre.

commentaries.author: Tertullian of Carthage
commentaries.content:
And title of brotherhood, and bond.
If we confess our sins, faithful and just is He to remit them to us, and utterly purify us from every unrighteousness."
commentaries.content_en:
And title of brotherhood, and bond.
If we confess our sins, faithful and just is He to remit them to us, and utterly purify us from every unrighteousness."
commentaries.content_pt:
E título de irmandade, e vínculo.
Se confessarmos nossos pecados, fiel e justo é Ele para nos remir e purificar completamente de toda injustiça.

commentaries.author: Theophylact of Ochrid
commentaries.content: It often occurs that the man who gives testimony is greater than the one of whom he testifies. Therefore, to prevent the evil thought that John, in bearing witness to Christ, was greater than Christ, the Evangelist says, He was not that Light. But could we not call John, or any one of the saints, a light? Yes, we may call every saint a light. But we cannot call them the Light, with the definite article. If someone says to you, "Surely John is a light?" agree with him. But if he says, "Surely John is the Light," say no. For he is not the very Light Itself, but a light by participation, which derives its brightness from the true Light.
commentaries.content_en: It often occurs that the man who gives testimony is greater than the one of whom he testifies. Therefore, to prevent the evil thought that John, in bearing witness to Christ, was greater than Christ, the Evangelist says, He was not that Light. But could we not call John, or any one of the saints, a light? Yes, we may call every saint a light. But we cannot call them the Light, with the definite article. If someone says to you, "Surely John is a light?" agree with him. But if he says, "Surely John is the Light," say no. For he is not the very Light Itself, but a light by participation, which derives its brightness from the true Light.
commentaries.content_pt: Ocorre frequentemente que o homem que dá testemunho é maior do que aquele de quem ele testemunha. Portanto, para prevenir o pensamento maligno de que João, ao dar testemunho de Cristo, era maior do que Cristo, o Evangelista diz: Ele não era essa Luz. Mas não poderíamos chamar João, ou qualquer um dos santos, de uma luz? Sim, podemos chamar cada santo de uma luz. Mas não podemos chamá-los de a Luz, com o artigo definido. Se alguém lhe disser: "Certamente João é uma luz?" concorde com ele. Mas se ele disser: "Certamente João é a Luz," diga não. Pois ele não é a própria Luz, mas uma luz por participação, que deriva seu brilho da verdadeira Luz.
